next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Doug took his pills in the basement bathroom with the door locked, which was unnecessary because Nate was upstairs — Doug could hear him through the floorboards, explaining something to someone, the specific pitch of Nate's voice when he wanted you to know he was handling it. The Lexapro first. He shook it out of the bottle and into his palm. Escitalopram oxalate, 10 milligrams, once daily with or without food, though the clinical data showed slightly better absorption with food and Doug hadn't eaten since yesterday's lunch. He swallowed it dry, the chalky scrape of it on the back of his tongue as familiar now as brushing his teeth. Three months. Same length of time as the divorce. The Lexapro arrived two weeks after Steph left, which meant his serotonin reuptake was being inhibited on roughly the same timeline as his marriage's dissolution, and he didn't know if the drug was working or if he was just getting used to the new shape of his life.

The Ativan was the other one. Lorazepam, 0.5 milligrams, as needed. It was needed. He'd sold both of these drugs for years — not these specific generics, the brand-names, back when the brand-names still mattered and pharmaceutical reps still got invited to lunch. He knew the dependency curve on benzodiazepines the way a bartender knows the ABV of draft beers: professional knowledge that had become uncomfortably personal. He swallowed the Ativan with a handful of water from the tap and studied his face in the mirror. Six feet of gaunt. Bags under his eyes that hadn't been there in June. The hoodie hanging off his shoulder...

prev_tail_excerpt:
...and offer some to whoever wanted it, because her mother was right: you didn't show up empty-handed, even to the worst places. Especially to the worst places.

She washed her water glass at the sink the way Decker had washed his mug, and she thought: everyone in this house is cleaning up after themselves. The question is what they're cleaning.

Outside, the late afternoon light was on the lake, turning it copper. She could see Whitfield sitting alone on the dock bench, sixty or seventy yards away through the pines and birch. He was sitting perfectly still, looking at the water. He might have been praying. He might have been sleeping with his eyes open. He might have been doing what she was doing — counting the things that didn't add up and coming up short of any total that made sense.

She put her notebook in her blazer pocket alongside the torn page and went to find her phone. She needed to call the BCA in St. Paul and ask about expediting the autopsy. She needed the cause of death, because everything she'd seen in this house said these men were hiding something, and she couldn't figure out what it was until she knew whether what they were hiding from was a murder or a natural death or just the specific unbearable thing that happened when your friend died in the night and you found yourself, at two in the morning, doing something with your hands that you couldn't explain.

The refrigerator cycled off. The house went quiet. Somewhere upstairs, the third step from the top of the stairs creaked — someone going up or coming down, she couldn't tell — and she wrote that down too.
